summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Dell's decision to offer Ubuntu Linux on their desktop computers, marking the first time the company has ever sold PCs without Windows pre-installed. He frames this as ending the 'Microsoft Tax' - the practice of forcing customers to pay for Windows whether they wanted it or not. By comparing pricing between identical Dell systems with Vista ($679) and Ubuntu ($599), he shows customers can save $80 by choosing Linux. Atwood praises Dell for playing 'fair and square' rather than charging extra for the non-Windows option, and suggests this moment could be a turning point in desktop computing history.
key_points: Dell offering Ubuntu marks the first time they've ever sold desktops without a Microsoft operating system
The 'Microsoft Tax' refers to being forced to pay for Windows on hardware purchases regardless of whether you want it
The price difference ($80) reveals Dell's actual cost for a Windows Vista license, which is cheaper than buying OEM separately ($129)
Offering a free OS is effectively the same as selling hardware without any operating system
This represents a win for consumer choice regardless of one's operating system preferences
money_quotes: Until today, it was quite literally impossible to decline the Windows license when you bought a desktop from Dell.
If you bought a desktop PC from Dell, you got – and paid for – a copy of Windows, whether you wanted it or not.
Offering a free desktop operating system is effectively the same thing as selling hardware without any operating system.
Whether you're a fan of the latest open source operating systems, or just a fan of plain old-fashioned consumer choice, the end of the Microsoft tax is a win for customers.
But if you have no intention of running Windows, you just saved eighty bucks.
It's also quite possible today will be looked back on as an important turning point in the history of desktop computing.
key_insight: Consumer choice in operating systems matters, and breaking Microsoft's bundling monopoly on major PC hardware represents a significant victory for both open source advocates and customers who simply want to pay only for what they use.
